generations.text:
My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you from the confines of this cold, windswept tower, where the sea howls like a vengeful spirit and the sky is a bruise of storm clouds. I have not penned a letter in five years, and yet, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself compelled to reach out, not only to the wind and the waves, but to you.

You were once my greatest joy, my pride, and the light that guided me through the darkest hours of my life. I have not spoken your name in so long, and now, as the gales grow louder, I fear I may not live to see another dawn. The lamp that has burned here for so many years is dimming, and I feel it in my bones that this may be the night it is snuffed out.

I have not been a good father, Eliza. I have let my duties to this tower, to this cursed sea, and to the ghosts of those who came before me, overshadow the love I once had for you. I have allowed my heart to harden, and in doing so, I have lost the very thing I most cherished.

But tonight, as the storm breaks upon us, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. I wonder if you still remember the way the sea looked at dusk, or if you recall the lullabies I used to hum to you as a child. I wonder if

**Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heart heavy with years of silence and regret that I take up my quill to write to you. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the sea has ever been a constant companion, it cannot match the ceaseless tide of sorrow that has swept through my days. I write to you now not as a man of stone, but as a father who has been driven to the edge of his soul by the weight of unspoken words.

The storm is coming, Eleanor. Not the gentle squall that graces the coast in spring, but a tempest such as this island has not seen in a decade. The winds howl like vengeful spirits, and the waves rise with the fury of a thousand wrongs. I have seen the sky darken as if the heavens themselves weep, and I fear that the sea may claim me before dawn. Yet even in this hour of peril, my thoughts turn not to the storm, but to you.

I know not what you have heard of me, nor what tales have passed through the lips of those who once called me father. I have been a man of duty, a sentinel of the cliffs, and I have not sought to be more. But I have not been a man of love, nor have I been a father as I should have been. I let pride and silence build a wall between

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now, not with the ease of a man who has long since ceased to care, but with the weight of a heart that still remembers you—your laughter, your tears, the way your eyes would light up when you spoke of your dreams. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written, not because I have forgotten, but because I have feared that what I might say would not be worth the words.

You were once my greatest joy, and now, in these quiet hours of the night, as the wind howls like a beast in the distance and the sea roars with a fury that seems to echo the sorrow within me, I find myself longing to reach out. I have spent these years in solitude, the lighthouse my only companion, the sea my only confidant. Yet, even in the silence, I hear your voice, and it stirs something deep within me that I have tried to bury.

The storm is upon us, Eliza. The sky is black as midnight, the waves rising like giants in their wrath. I have seen many storms in my time, but this one—this one feels different. The wind is not just a force of nature, but a reminder of the storms in my soul. I do not know if I will see the dawn, and if I do, I fear I may not be the man you remember.

I have not spoken of you to

**[Envelope Addressed in a Handwritten Script]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**From Your Father,**

**At the Lighthouse,**

**On the Rocks,**

**April 5th, 1897**

---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

It is with a heavy heart that I take up my pen, for I have not written to you in five long years. Time, as it often does, has moved on without you, and I find myself standing at the edge of the sea, with the storm gathering on the horizon. I write not with the hope of mending what has been broken between us, but with the hope that, should I not return from this night, these words may reach you and perhaps, in some small way, bring us back together.

You were always a spirited child, Eleanor. I remember the way you would run up the stairs of the lighthouse, your laughter echoing through the stone walls, and how you would ask me questions about the stars and the sea, as though they held the answers to life’s greatest mysteries. You had a curious mind, and I often wished I could have shared more of my own wisdom with you. But perhaps that was my fault, for I was too proud to speak of my own failings, and too stubborn to admit that I had not been the best of fathers.

The world has changed in the five years since you left. The lighthouse, once a place of

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of a damned soul, and the sea roiling as if it seeks to swallow the very earth. The lighthouse stands, though I fear it may not for long. The storm is upon us, and I am not certain I shall see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you turned your back on me, on this place, on the life we once shared. I have tried to forget, but the memories cling like the salt on my skin. The sound of your laughter, the way you used to dance in the rain, the way you looked at me with those eyes—clear, bright, full of promise. And then, the silence. The silence that followed. The silence that has grown louder with each passing day.

I have been alone here, save for the sea and the wind and the ever-watchful light. I have kept the lamp burning, not because I fear the dark, but because I fear what might happen if I let it go. The light is a promise, Eliza. A promise that I will not abandon my duty. A promise that I will not abandon this place. A promise that I will not abandon *you*.

I do not know if I will survive this night. The waves are rising, the sky is black, and the wind is not kind. But if I do, I will not let this silence

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the lighthouse walls. The sea roars, and the sky is a mass of fury, black and churning. I’ve spent my life standing here, guiding ships through the darkness, and now I fear I may not be able to guide myself through this storm.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unanswered, of the weight of your absence pressing down on my soul like the tide. I’ve tried to forget, to move on, to pretend that I never held you in my arms, never sang you lullabies, never watched you grow from a little girl into a woman who turned her back on me.

But tonight, I can’t pretend. Tonight, the sea is restless, and I feel it in my bones. I’ve seen the sky change—those ominous clouds, the way the wind shifts, the way the waves grow taller, as if the sea itself is warning me. I’ve kept the light burning for years, but I fear it may not be enough this time.

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. You were the reason I kept this lighthouse, the reason I never gave up. You were my daughter, my heart, and I was the fool who let you go.

I’ve thought of you often—when the storms were calm, when the days were long and the nights

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart both heavy and full, for the wind howls like a banshee upon the cliffs, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this lonely keep. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet, in this moment, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, my child, my daughter, my only tether to the world beyond these walls.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written, nor have you replied. I know you have been gone from this place, from this life, and perhaps from me. I do not blame you. I have not been a father as I should have been. I have been a man of duty, of routine, of the light that guides the lost and the weary. I have been a lighthouse keeper, and not a father.

You were a child when I last saw you, and I was a man who had long since forgotten how to be a father. I thought I could fill the hours with the sea, the wind, the light, and the solitude of this place. I thought the silence would keep me company. But it was not silence—it was absence. And I have carried that absence like a stone upon my chest.

I have not spoken of you, not in words, not in letters. I have not spoken of the days when you would sit by

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1887**

**Dear Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I daresay I have little to offer in the way of good news. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though I have not forgotten you, the world has shifted in ways I had not foreseen. The sea, my constant companion, has grown more restless of late, and I fear the tempest that now approaches may be one from which I shall not return.

I write to you not with the hope of rekindling the past, but with the earnest desire to let you know that I have not forgotten you, nor have I ceased to think of you. You were once the light of my life, and though the years have dulled the edges of our bond, I still carry the memory of your laughter, your kindness, and the warmth of your presence in my heart.

I have lived in this lighthouse for many years now, and though the work is arduous, it is the duty I have taken upon myself. The light burns with unwavering vigilance, as it must, for the safety of those who sail upon these waters. I have always believed that the light is not merely a beacon for ships, but a symbol of hope and guidance for all who dwell in the dark. I hope you have found such guidance in your own life.

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay I can’t say the same for myself. It’s been five years since you left, and I’ve spent those years counting the hours between the tides, the hours between the storms, the hours between the moments I feel like I’m losing you. I’ve been a lighthouse keeper for twenty now, and I’ve seen more storms than I can count, but tonight, I’m not sure I’ll make it through.

The wind is already howling like a beast, and the sea is restless. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but I can’t shake the feeling that something is wrong. The waves are coming in faster, higher, and I’ve never seen the sea act this way before. I’ve been here through typhoons and gales, but this… this feels different. Like the storm is alive, and it knows I’m alone.

I’ve been meaning to write to you for so long, but the words have always felt too heavy. I’ve tried to tell myself that you were better off without me, that I was a fool to think I could be anything more than a father who failed you. But now, as I sit here in the cold, the lamp’s light flickering against the storm, I wonder if I’ve ever truly been able to let go.

You were just a child when you left, Eleanor. I remember the day

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five long years have passed since the last time I saw you, and though the days have dragged on like the endless hours of a storm, I have never once stopped thinking of you. The sea has been my only companion, and now, as the wind howls and the sky darkens with a fury I have never seen, I find myself wondering if this storm will claim me before I can speak these words to you.

You were not the same after the day you left. I remember the way you stood at the door, your eyes filled with something I could not name—anger, sorrow, or perhaps both. You said you needed to see the world beyond these rocks, that you wanted to be someone more than what I could offer. I did not argue. I simply let you go. And I have never stopped regretting it.

The lighthouse has been my life, my duty, my burden. I have kept the light burning through every storm, every night, every season. But tonight is different. The sky is black as ink, the wind screams like a banshee, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a violence that shakes the very foundations of this old tower. I have never felt so alone. Not even the sea can calm my thoughts now.

I have thought of you often. I have imagined your face, your voice, the way you used to

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The sea howls beyond the cliffs, its fury echoing the tempest within me. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I've known, and I fear the waves may claim more than the rocks below. Yet, in this moment of uncertainty, I find myself thinking of you—my beloved daughter, my only child.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not been the same since. The light of the lighthouse has grown dim, not because of the wind or the rain, but because my soul has been shadowed by the weight of what we could not say. I have not spoken of you in years, not because I have forgotten you, but because I feared that every word I spoke would only bring more pain. And yet, here I am, writing to you now, as if hoping that the storm might break, and with it, the walls I have built around my heart.

I know you have your own life, your own path, and I do not wish to intrude upon it. But I must tell you this: I have not stopped thinking of you. Every night, as I stand at the top of the tower, I see the light flicker, and I think of the light you once brought into my life—your laughter, your kindness, your spirit. You were my sun, Eliza, and even now,

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink still wet from the last candle I lit, and the wind howling like a banshee in the distance. The sea, my dear, has never been so restless. The waves crash against the cliffs like a thousand angry souls, and I fear the night will not end until the rocks are torn asunder. Yet I write, for I know you will not hear my voice in this storm, and I must say these things before the sea swallows me whole.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long, bitter years. I have not seen you, nor heard your voice, nor felt your hand upon my shoulder. You were once my light in the darkness, as the lighthouse is to the sailors. Now, that light is gone, and I am left with only the flicker of a dying lamp and the sound of the wind.

I have often wondered if you remember the nights we spent by the hearth, when you were a child and I would read you stories of the sea. You used to laugh when I spoke of the great leviathans that lurk in the deep, and you would press your small hands to your cheeks, wide-eyed with wonder. I miss that. I miss the way your laughter would echo through the stone walls of this old keep, like the sound of the tide on the shore.

But now, I am alone. The loneliness is a thing

My Dearest Eleanor,

It has been five long years since I last laid eyes upon you, and yet the weight of your absence has grown heavier with each passing season. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the desperate need to be heard, to be remembered, and to be understood.

The sea has been restless of late, as it often is when the heart is troubled. Tonight, a storm is gathering on the horizon—a tempest of such ferocity that even the oldest of the cliffs here tremble in its wake. I have stood upon the tower, as I have done for many years, watching the dark clouds churn and the winds howl like the ghosts of those who have come before me. I fear I may not see dawn.

I have always been a man of quiet resolve, Eleanor. I have stood alone in this place, weathering the storms of the sea and the storms of the soul. But this storm feels different. It does not merely threaten the land; it threatens the very soul of this lighthouse. I do not know if I shall survive it, and I do not know if you will ever forgive me for the silence that has stretched between us.

You were once my greatest joy, my guiding star in a world that often felt too vast and cruel for a single soul to bear. I remember the days when you would come to visit, your laughter echoing through the halls like the sound of waves against the

**The Lighthouse Keeper’s Letter**  
*October 12th, 1887*  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

I write this with a hand that trembles not from fear, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five years have passed since you left this house, and though the sea may roar and the wind may howl, it cannot match the silence that has settled between us.  

You were a child when you ran from me, and I was a man too proud, too stubborn, to chase you. I told myself that time would mend what was broken, that the sea would carry your anger away. But time has only deepened the rift, and the sea—ah, the sea—has never forgotten.  

Tonight, the storm comes. The sky is black as ink, and the wind has begun to howl like a beast unleashed. The waves claw at the rocks, and the lighthouse stands alone, a sentinel against the chaos. I have kept the light burning for decades, and I have no doubt that I shall keep it tonight. But I fear that the light may not be enough to guide you back to me.  

I do not write this to beg for your forgiveness, for I know that words are as frail as the lantern’s glass. Yet I must say this: I have not forgotten the days when you used to run up the stairs to see the sea at dawn, when you would press your small hand against the

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea raging as if it seeks to swallow the very stones that hold our lighthouse firm. Tonight, the skies are dark as ink, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones quake. I have not seen the dawn in five years, and I fear the dawn may never come again.

You were but a child when you left this place, and I have been a solitary figure ever since, tending to the light, the lantern, the books, and the ghosts of the past. I have kept the lighthouse lit through every storm, every gale, every tempest that has threatened to tear it down. But now, as the tempest gathers strength, I am not certain if I shall be here to see the dawn. I have been told that the sea can be cruel, and that in such storms, even the strongest of men may be claimed by the tide.

You were always a spirited child, full of dreams and a restless heart. I remember the day you left, your eyes filled with fire, your voice with defiance. "You are too old to understand," you said, and I believed you. I did not know then that the world was far greater than the horizon, and that the tides of life are as unpredictable as the sea.

I have often wondered what became of you. I have

**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of the Lighthouse of Fairhaven]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that old and ceaseless companion, roars with a fury I have not seen in many a year. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may not spare me.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written to you, nor have you to me. I know you would call it cowardice, or perhaps even neglect. But I have been a man of duty, Eleanor, and the light must always burn, even when the world forgets it. I have not forgotten you, though.

I have often thought of you, my dear child, and the days when we walked the cliffs together, the sea at our feet, the wind in our hair. You were a bright light in my life, and I have carried that light with me, even when the world grew dark.

I do not know if I will survive this night. The storm has already claimed the lives of two men who sailed through these waters last week. I have seen the sea rage and the sky darken, and I have felt the weight of the world upon my shoulders. But I have also felt the warmth of your memory, and it has been a comfort to me.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet it feels as though it were only yesterday. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and I do not know if I ever will again. But tonight, as the tempest gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in years. Not with anger, though I have had ample cause for it, but with a sorrow that has long since settled into the bones of me.  

You were a child when you ran away, Eliza. A child with fire in her heart and a mind too sharp for her years. I tried to understand, to reach you, but you would not listen. You called me a tyrant, a brute, a man who had forgotten how to love. And perhaps I did. Perhaps I had forgotten. Or perhaps I had never truly known.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I know you won’t. You’ve never been one for letters, and I’ve never been one for writing them. But tonight, the wind howls like a beast in the night, and I feel it in my bones that this may be the last time I can write to you.

Five years have passed since you left, and five years have passed since I stopped speaking. You told me I was cruel, that I never listened, that I never saw you. I suppose I didn’t. I was too busy watching the sea, too busy keeping the light burning, too busy pretending that the silence between us was just the sound of the waves.

But tonight, the silence feels different. The storm is coming, and I fear I may not make it through the night. The lighthouse has stood for over a century, but I’ve never felt so small, so fragile, as I do now. The wind is like a hand on my shoulder, and the sea is rising, as if it, too, is trying to reach me.

I’ve always believed that the lighthouse was a beacon—not just for ships, but for those who had lost their way. Perhaps I was that beacon for you, and I failed. I failed to see you, to understand you, to hold you. I thought I was protecting you, but in the end, I only pushed

It has been five long years since I last laid eyes upon you, and the weight of that silence sits heavy upon my heart like the stone walls of this lighthouse. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the desperate need to speak, to be heard, before the storm that now rages upon the horizon swallows me whole.

The sea has always been my companion, my guide, my master. It has carried me through the years, and now it calls to me once more, with a fury that I fear I shall not survive. The wind howls like a wounded beast, the waves crash with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this tower. I have seen storms before, but never one like this. I fear I may not make it through the night.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not. It has been five years since you left, and I have spent each day since in the company of the sea, its relentless waves and ceaseless howl a balm to my weary soul. Yet tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sky darkens with a storm that feels almost divine in its fury, I find myself thinking of you more than I ever have.

You were always a storm in your own right, my girl. I remember the day you left—your eyes were like winter, cold and unyielding. You told me you needed to find yourself, that you couldn’t stay in the shadow of this lighthouse, this life, this me. I didn’t argue. I didn’t know how. I only watched as you walked away, your back straight, your hands clenched at your sides. I’ve never seen you so certain of your path, and yet so uncertain of where it would lead.

I’ve been alone here for years now, save for the lighthouse keeper who passed last winter. He left me a note, a simple one: *“The light still burns. The sea still calls.”* I suppose that’s all I have left to anchor me. The light, the sea, and the silence between them. I’ve grown used to the solitude, but tonight... tonight feels different

I write to you now, not from a place of anger or bitterness, but from a place of quiet longing and the weight of a storm that threatens to consume us all.

Tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself staring at the horizon, wondering if I will see another dawn. The waves grow wilder, the lighthouse flickers uncertainly, and I feel the weight of the years between us pressing down like the tide.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, I have come to understand the pain I caused you. I was blind to the truth of your sorrow, to the silence you carried like a shroud. I was too proud, too stubborn, and too afraid to reach out. I let my pride blind me to the love that still lingers in my heart, even now, in this moment of uncertainty.

I have spent these years watching the sea, not just as a keeper of the light, but as a man who has learned to see the light within myself. I have come to understand that forgiveness is not something you give—it is something you receive, and I have been waiting for you to grant me that gift. But I fear it may be too late. **To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in the arms of some doting suitor or perhaps in a fine London townhouse, surrounded by the trappings of society. I write this on a night thick with the scent of brine and the groan of the sea, as the wind howls like a wounded beast. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than just the lighthouse.

Five years, Eliza. Five years since you left me with a curt note and a heart like a shattered mirror. I have not seen you since the day you stormed out, your voice ringing in my ears like a bell of doom. You were so angry, so full of righteous fury, and I understand now that I was not the one who was wrong. I was a fool, a man who clung to the past like a drowning man to driftwood, and you were right to leave me. But I have spent these years thinking of you, and I have come to see how much I have lost.

You were always the light in my life, my little star. I used to tell you stories of the sea, of the great beasts that lurk beneath the waves, of the storms that rage and the calm that follows. I would show you the stars, and we would dream of the world beyond this lonely rock. But you grew up, and your dreams were no longer

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you from the very heart of the storm, where the sea howls like a wounded beast and the sky weeps in torrents. The lighthouse stands firm, as it always has, though I fear this night may test even its unyielding strength. I have not penned a letter in five years, nor have I spoken your name aloud in the silence of these stone walls, but the wind has carried your voice to me tonight, and I must write.

I have been a fool, Eliza. I have let the sea and the silence between us grow too wide. I have thought myself a man of duty, a guardian of the coast, yet I have neglected the greatest duty of all—that to my own child. I have not been there when you needed me, and for that, I am truly sorry.

The storm is upon us now, and the waves rise with a fury I have not seen since the day you left. I have always believed that the light must burn, that the beacon must guide, but tonight I wonder if I am not the one who needs the guidance. I have been so consumed by the task of keeping the light that I have forgotten how to keep the heart.

**April 12th, 1878**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

The storm that now looms on the horizon is not merely a tempest of wind and rain—it is a storm of emotions, of memories, and of unspoken words. I write to you not only as a father, but as a man who has spent his life watching the sea, and who knows that even the mightiest waves must one day recede.

The fire has long since gone out, and I’ve come to think that perhaps the light was never meant to warm the heart, only to guide the lost.

**October 23rd, 1892**  
**Lighthouse, Blackmoor Point, Cornwall**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy with both sorrow and resolve, for the winds howl with a fury that has not been seen in these parts for many a year. The sea, that old and fickle companion, has turned its wrath upon us, and the storm that approaches with the rising tide threatens to claim more than just the rocks and the waves—it threatens the very soul of this lonely keep.

I do not know if you are well, or if you have found happiness beyond the confines of our once-quiet life. I pray that you have. I pray, too, that you have not been driven to the same despair that has taken root in my own heart.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know the chances of it reaching you are slim. It’s been five years since we last spoke, and I’ve come to accept that words between us have long since turned to dust. You were always the storm in my life, my Eleanor. You were the light that refused to be dimmed, even when I tried to drown it out. I remember the night you left—how the rain fell in sheets, how your eyes were filled with fire, and how I could not stop you. You said you were going to find your own path, and I let you go. I thought I was doing the right thing. I thought I was protecting you. But now, as I sit here with the lamp in my hands, I wonder if I was only protecting myself.

The storm is coming. I’ve been watching the sky all day, the way the clouds churn like ink spilled across the heavens. The waves are rising, and the lighthouse is old. I’ve kept it running for twenty years, but I fear it may not hold. I’ve spent my life keeping the light burning, and tonight, I may not be able to keep it alive.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside this crumbling tower, and the sea roiling as though it knows the weight of my heart. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself haunted by memories of you—your laughter, your dreams, the way you once used to run through the dunes, chasing the horizon with eyes full of wonder.

I have spent these years in solitude, tending to this lighthouse, the only company being the flickering light and the ever-present sea. It is a lonely existence, but it has kept me grounded, if only in the smallest way. And now, as the storm threatens to swallow us whole, I feel the weight of my silence more than ever.

I have longed to write to you, to tell you of the days that have passed, of the moments I have cherished and the ones I have mourned. But I feared that my words would not be enough, that they would fall short of the love and regret I carry. You were always my light, Eleanor—my guiding star, my greatest hope. And I have let you go, not because I no longer care, but because I feared that I could not offer you the life you deserved.

I have watched you grow from a little girl with wide eyes and a heart full of dreams to a woman I could never claim as my own.

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea, that eternal beast, roars in protest against the heavens. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me before I can finish this. But I must—because I have something to say, and if the sea swallows me, I want the words to reach you.

I have tried to write to you, but the sea has always been too loud, and the ink would dry before I could finish a sentence. It seems the ocean has its own way of keeping secrets, just like you.

You remember the night I told you I would never leave this lighthouse? That I would stand guard until the day I died, and that I would keep the light burning for you? I meant it, Eliza. I truly did. But the world is not kind to those who hold on too tightly, and I have come to understand that even the strongest of men can be broken by the weight of their own love.
